results.query: What are the six stages of the ML/AI lifecycle?
results.answer:
The provided context does not explicitly list the six stages of the ML/AI lifecycle, but it is implied that they will be covered in the slides. However, since the question asks for the stages, I would infer that the six stages are likely to be:

1. Data Collection (implied from Chunk 3 - "Monitoring feeds back into data collection")
2. Data Preparation
3. Model Training
4. Model Validation
5. Deployment
6. Monitoring and Maintenance (implicit from Chunk 3 - "Monitoring feeds back into data collection" and Chunk 1 - "The Six Lifecycle Stages")

This inference is supported by the context, as these stages are common in many machine learning lifecycles and align with the information provided about the circular nature of the ML/AI lifecycle. However, without explicit confirmation from the slides, this answer should be considered an educated guess.

results.query: Why is the ML lifecycle described as circular rather than linear?
results.answer: The ML lifecycle is described as circular rather than linear because it is a continuous process where monitoring feeds back into data collection, as mentioned in Chunk 3 (Page 91). This means that after deploying a model, the results are monitored and the insights gained can be used to improve the data collection process for future iterations.

results.query: What does Git track well in ML projects and what does it not track efficiently?
results.answer: In ML projects, Git tracks code changes to training scripts and inference services, configuration file updates (YAML, JSON), documentation revisions, and dependency specifications. However, it does not track large model weights or datasets efficiently. For these, it is recommended to use DVC or Git LFS instead.

retrieved_chunks.text:
What is Git? (cont.)
Git is a distributed version control system that tracks changes to files over time.
For ML projects, Git tracks:
• Code changes to training scripts and inference services
• Configuration file updates (Y AML, JSON)
• Documentation revisions
• Dependency specifications
Git does NOT eﬀiciently track:
• Large model weights
• Datasets (use DVC or Git LFS)
￿ See: Git Fundamentals for MLOps
18

Git’s Three States (cont.)
￿ See: Git Fundamentals for MLOps

gitinit
gitclone https://github.com/username/repo.git
# Stage and commit
gitadd .
gitcommit-m"Add prediction endpoint"
# Branch and merge

gitmerge feature-branch
# Push and pull
gitpush origin main

￿ See: Git Fundamentals for MLOps22

results.query: Why do virtual environments matter in MLOps?
results.answer: Virtual environments matter in MLOps for several reasons as stated on pages 29 and 30 of the provided slide deck. They help to avoid dependency conflicts, provide isolation per project, ensure a reproducible setup, prevent global package pollution, control version chaos, and maintain clean, controlled dependencies by pinning versions. Additionally, virtual environments are created and activated via the command line (CLI), which is crucial for MLOps as mentioned on page 13 of the slide deck.

Why Virtual Environments Matter (cont.)

Without Virtual Environments With Virtual Environments
Dependency conflicts Isolation per project
“Works on my machine” Reproducible setup
Global package pollution Clean, controlled deps
Version chaos Pinned versions
26

Command Line Basics (cont.)
Why CLI matters for MLOps:
• Git is primarily a command-line tool
• Virtual environments are created and activated via CLI
• CI pipelines execute shell commands
• Docker and Kubernetes are controlled via CLI
• Remote servers typically only have CLI access

results.query: What is dependency pinning and why is it important for reproducibility?
results.answer:
Dependency pinning refers to the practice of specifying exact versions of packages in a software project. This is done to ensure that the same code will produce the same results every time, regardless of any changes made to the underlying libraries or packages.

Dependency pinning is important for reproducibility because machine learning systems are sensitive to version changes. Even minor updates to libraries like NumPy can alter numerical results, while upgrades to scikit-learn can change model behavior. By using exact versions of dependencies, we can ensure that the same code will always produce the same output.

(Citations: Chunk 1 from page 36, Chunk 2 from page 37, and Chunk 4 from page 81 in the provided context)

retrieved_chunks.text:
What is Dependency Pinning? (cont.)
Dependency pinning = specifying exact versions of packages
# WRONG: Version ranges
numpy>=1.20
pandas>=1.3
# CORRECT: Exact versions
numpy==1.24.3
pandas==2.0.3
￿ See: Dependency Pinning & Reproducibility
32

Why Pinning Matters (cont.)
# Generate pinned requirements
pip freeze >requirements.txt
# Install from pinned file
pip install -rrequirements.txt
ML systems are sensitive to version changes:
• A minor NumPy update can alter numerical results
• A scikit-learn upgrade can change model behavior
• Same code + different versions = different results
￿ See: Dependency Pinning & Reproducibility
33

Reproducibility and
Dependencies

Demo: Expected Output (cont.)

results.query: What does the sample GitHub Actions workflow do?
results.answer: The sample GitHub Actions workflow, as shown in Chunk 3, is a basic Continuous Integration (CI) workflow. It automatically runs when there's a push or pull request on the 'main' branch. The workflow sets up an environment using Ubuntu-latest, checks out the code, installs Python version 3.10, and its dependencies specified in requirements.txt, then runs tests on the 'tests/' directory. If successful, it reports a pass status; if not, it reports a fail status. (Referenced pages: 49, 52, 54, 55)

retrieved_chunks.text:
What is GitHub Actions? (cont.)
GitHub Actions = CI/CD platform built into GitHub
• Automatically runs workflows on push/PR events
• Sets up fresh environments
• Installs dependencies
• Runs tests
• Reports pass/fail status
￿ See: GitHub Actions & CI Basics
47

Basic CI Workflow (cont.)
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name :CI
on :
push :
branches : [main ]
pull_request :
branches : [main ]
jobs :
test :
runs-on :ubuntu-latest
steps :
-uses :actions/checkout@v4
-uses :actions/setup-python@v5
with :
python-version :"3.10"
-run :pip install -r requirements.txt
-run :pytest tests/ -v
￿ See: GitHub Actions & CI Basics49

results.query: What are artifacts in ML pipelines? Give some examples.
results.answer: Artifacts in Machine Learning (ML) pipelines refer to any files produced during the ML workflow that need to be saved. Examples of artifacts include trained model files such as .pkl, .h5, and .pt; preprocessor objects like scalers and encoders; feature definitions and schemas; evaluation metrics and reports (referenced on pages 62-64 in the provided context).

retrieved_chunks.text: Pipelines and Artifacts

What are Pipelines and Artifacts? (cont.)
Data Pipeline: Sequence of processing steps that transforms raw data
Artifact: Any file produced during ML workflow that needs to be saved
• T rained model files ( .pkl ,.h5 ,.pt )
• Preprocessor objects (scalers, encoders)
• Feature definitions and schemas
• Evaluation metrics and reports
￿ See: Data Pipelines & Model Artifacts

results.query: How do traditional ML systems differ from LLM systems?
results.answer:
Traditional Machine Learning (ML) systems and Large Language Models (LLMs) differ in several aspects.

1. Model source: Traditional ML models are typically trained in-house, while LLMs are pre-trained via API.
2. Data requirements: ML systems require labeled training data, whereas LLMs use prompts and few-shot examples.
3. Customization: Feature engineering is used for customization in traditional ML, while prompt engineering is used for LLMs.
4. Artifacts: Model files are the artifacts produced by traditional ML, while prompt templates are the artifacts for LLMs.
5. Inference cost: The inference cost for traditional ML is predictable, whereas it is variable (depending on token count) for LLMs.
6. Latency: Inference latency for traditional ML is measured in milliseconds, while it can be seconds for LLMs.
7. Task versatility: Traditional ML models are designed for one specific task, while LLMs can perform many tasks via prompting. (Reference pages 69-71)

retrieved_chunks.text:
ML vs LLM Comparison (cont.)
￿ See: ML vs LLM System Architectures
64

ML vs LLM Comparison (cont.)
Aspect T raditional ML LLM Systems
Model source T rained in-house Pre-trained via API
Data requirements Labeled training data Prompts, few-shot examples
Customization Feature engineering Prompt engineering
Artifacts Model files Prompt templates
Inference cost Predictable Variable (token count)
Latency Milliseconds Seconds
63

What is an LLM? (cont.)
Large Language Model (LLM): Neural network trained on massive text data
• Generates coherent text by predicting next token
• Follows natural language instructions
• Generalizes to tasks not explicitly trained for
# Traditional ML: One model, one task
spam_classifier.predict( "Free money!" ) # "spam"
# LLM: One model, many tasks via prompting
llm.generate( "Classify as spam: 'Free money!'" )
llm.generate( "Translate to Spanish: 'Hello'" )
￿ See: ML vs LLM System

)
llm.generate( "Translate to Spanish: 'Hello'" )

results.query: What kinds of technical debt are mentioned in ML systems and how can they be prevented?
results.answer:
In Machine Learning (ML) systems, the following types of technical debt are mentioned:

1. Dependency Debt: This occurs due to hidden data dependencies. To prevent this, it is recommended to document data contracts.

2. Configuration Debt: This arises from scattered settings. Prevention strategies include centralizing configurations and setting up Continuous Integration (CI).

3. Data Testing Debt: This results from untested data assumptions. Schema validation can help prevent this.

To prevent these debts, the practices established in Milestone 0 should include pinning dependencies, using version control, setting up CI, and documenting data contracts.
